title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Bayesian Functional Mixed Model with Norm-Preserving Warping

## Summary

This paper develops a Bayesian functional mixed model framework that employs norm-preserving warping for functional data analysis. The work makes meaningful contributions to the field through clear mathematical exposition and practical computational strategies. However, the evaluation methodology raises concerns regarding fair comparison and practical validation. The assessment relies predominantly on visual comparisons against a single baseline without addressing fundamental differences in model specifications or demonstrating downstream utility.

## Strengths

The paper excels in its pedagogical presentation of temporal warping models. The mathematical exposition clearly delineates between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions, supported by both intuitive explanations and illuminating visual comparisons. This treatment is well-grounded in historical context, drawing thoughtful parallels to established work in 2D shape analysis that effectively motivate the proposed extensions to functional data.

The authors demonstrate solid technical competence in implementing infinite-dimensional computations through basis function projections, specifically using B-splines. This practical approach to handling high-dimensional functional data is sensible and well-executed. The work is timely and well-positioned to contribute meaningfully to functional data analysis as these methods find increasing application across domains.

## Major Weaknesses

**Comparison Fairness and Baseline Selection**: The evaluation is substantially limited by comparison against only a single publicly available implementation (warpMix) using default parameters. More critically, there appears to be a fundamental mismatch in noise variance specifications between the proposed model (σ²_c = 0.25, σ² = 1) and warpMix (~10⁻³ level)—a difference of several orders of magnitude. This disparity raises serious questions about whether the comparison is equitable. The authors do not attempt to align these assumptions or provide explicit justification for why such different specifications should be considered comparable.

**Limited Engagement with Related Work**: The paper inadequately addresses related work by Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues who have published similar norm-preserving action models for estimating mean functions from functional data. This is a notable gap given that the core technical contribution appears to overlap substantially with existing literature. The paper would benefit from more thorough positioning relative to these approaches and explicit discussion of novel contributions beyond prior work.

**Evaluation Methodology**: The evaluation relies almost exclusively on subjective visual assessment without quantifiable performance metrics. Claims about estimate quality—such as statements that recovered features are "sharp" and "representative"—lack objective validation. More fundamentally, there is no evaluation on downstream tasks (e.g., classification, clustering, or other practical applications) that would demonstrate the actual utility of the recovered parameters in realistic settings.

## Technical Concerns

The substantial difference in noise variance parameters between models deserves explicit attention. If the authors believe their specification is more appropriate, this should be justified theoretically or empirically. Alternatively, sensitivity analysis or parameter matching should be attempted to ensure fair comparison.

The paper mentions that data-driven functional PCA bases (explored in the appendix) performed worse than B-splines, but the implications of this choice are not thoroughly discussed. Questions remain about the practical consequences of assuming specific basis function families versus direct sample-based representations, and what simplifications or complications would emerge from avoiding basis function approximations altogether.

## Minor Suggestions

1. Attempt to match noise variance assumptions between the proposed model and warpMix, or provide explicit justification for the differences and their impact on comparability.

2. Include comparisons with other norm-preserving action models from the related literature, particularly work by Kurtek and Srivastava.

3. Supplement visual assessments with objective evaluation criteria and quantitative metrics.

4. Evaluate the method on real downstream tasks to demonstrate practical utility beyond parameter recovery.

5. Provide deeper discussion of basis function selection and its implications for the method's applicability.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes solid contributions to functional data analysis through clear exposition of norm-preserving warping models and practical computational implementations. The mathematical framework is sound and well-presented. However, the work falls short of publication standards for a top venue due to methodological limitations in its evaluation. The comparison against a single baseline with substantially different model specifications, limited engagement with related work, and reliance on subjective visual assessment without downstream task validation collectively undermine confidence in the practical utility and novelty of the approach. With revisions addressing the fairness of comparisons, broader baseline engagement, and quantitative downstream evaluation, this work could make a stronger contribution.

## Recommendation

**Accept with Major Revisions** (Score: 7/10)

The paper merits publication given its clear exposition and technical soundness, but requires substantial improvements to the evaluation methodology before acceptance. The authors must address the fairness of baseline comparisons, engage more thoroughly with related work, and provide objective validation through downstream tasks.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).

## Novelty:

Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: [Paper Title]

## Summary

This paper presents a carefully formulated approach to shape analysis using size-and-shape preserving transformations with phase variability. While the manuscript demonstrates clear presentation and rigorous mathematical development, significant concerns regarding practical significance, methodological justification, and comparative evaluation limit its contribution.

## Strengths

The paper is exceptionally well-written and self-contained, with a level of clarity that distinguishes it from much recent work in this area. The authors deserve credit for systematically justifying their modeling choices, particularly in the selection of basis functions and prior distributions. The experimental section is comprehensive, combining synthetic and real data experiments that provide reasonable coverage of the proposed method's behavior.

## Major Weaknesses

**1. Unclear Practical Significance**

The primary concern is whether the size-and-shape preserving formulation addresses genuine practical needs. The paper does not adequately motivate why this specific constraint is important for real applications, nor does it clearly articulate when practitioners should adopt this approach over simpler alternatives. This foundational question undermines the work's impact regardless of technical merit.

**2. Insufficiently Justified Modeling Choices**

The decision to employ phase-preserving rather than value-preserving actions on the fixed effect μ lacks adequate justification. The explanation in lines 142-147 is particularly problematic: it claims that equivalence classes under norm-preserving actions are "larger" than those under value-preserving actions, but this argument is presented in a hand-wavy manner without rigorous proof or formal references. More critically, the counterargument presented—that one can find equivalent f for any g and γ—applies equally to value-preserving actions, thereby undermining the entire justification for the chosen approach. The authors should either provide a rigorous proof of the claimed size difference or reconsider the fundamental motivation for this choice.

**3. Limited Real Data Validation**

Figure 4's comparison with warpMix is problematic because the absence of ground truth makes it nearly impossible to assess which method is superior. Visual inspection alone is insufficient evidence, and the paper provides no quantitative metrics or additional validation that would convincingly demonstrate the proposed method's advantages on real data. This substantially weakens claims about practical utility.

**4. Incomplete Treatment of Design Choices**

The constraint introduced at line 76 is presented without discussion of its optimality or viable alternatives. How does this choice compare to other normalization schemes? How might different constraints handle noise or outliers? The paper should either justify why this particular constraint is optimal or acknowledge its limitations and discuss alternatives.

**5. Novelty Concerns**

The relationship to existing work in geometric vision—particularly landmark-based shape analysis and methods for handling equivalence classes and non-minimal parameterizations—is not thoroughly discussed. The paper does not adequately articulate what is genuinely novel relative to established approaches in this domain.

## Minor Weaknesses and Technical Issues

- The motivation for introducing phase variability is unclear in the introduction. Why is this extension necessary? Under what conditions is it reasonable to assume phase functions act in a phase-preserving manner?

- The relationship between the general model (1) and the more complex model with phase variability needs clarification. Why is model (1) insufficient?

- Line 119 contains what appears to be a notation issue ("gamma in Gamma?") that should be corrected.

- The reference to Example 6.5.2 in Bogachev (1998) in Footnote 1 is cited but not developed with sufficient rigor.

- The paper employs MCMC for parameter estimation but provides no comparison with optimization-based alternatives or discussion of the relative merits of these approaches.

- The assumption of common discretization and Gaussian errors may not hold across all applications and deserves acknowledgment.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Add a preamble to Section 2 that clearly explains why the properties of phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are essential to solving the problem at hand.

2. Provide rigorous mathematical justification (or formal references) for the claim about equivalence class sizes, or reconsider the fundamental approach.

3. Discuss how the constraint at line 76 was selected and compare it rigorously to plausible alternatives.

4. Expand the discussion of connections to geometric vision literature, particularly work on handling equivalence classes under group actions.

5. Strengthen real data experiments through either: (a) applications with ground truth, or (b) more convincing indirect evidence of superiority over existing methods like warpMix.

6. Include discussion of when phase-preserving versus value-preserving actions are appropriate, with concrete examples.

7. Discuss the trade-offs between MCMC and optimization-based approaches for parameter estimation.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid technical contribution with excellent presentation quality. However, the practical significance of the proposed approach remains unclear, key modeling decisions lack rigorous justification, and real-world validation is insufficient. The work reads more as a methodological exercise than a solution to a compelling practical problem. While the mathematical development is sound and the experiments are reasonably comprehensive, the combination of unclear motivation, questionable design choices, and weak comparative validation prevents this from being a strong contribution.

The paper would benefit from substantial revision addressing the practical significance and justification issues before publication. With significant effort to clarify motivation, provide rigorous justification for design choices, and strengthen real data validation, this could become a solid contribution to the field.

**Recommendation: Accept with Major Revisions** (Rating: 7)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

Weak comparison with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) - difficult to assess which method is better without ground truth

The counterargument in lines 142-147 can be applied equally to value-preserving action, undermining the justification

No discussion of whether the chosen constraint is optimal or how other alternatives might handle noise or outliers differently
suggestions: Provide more rigorous proof or references for why equivalence classes under norm-preserving action are larger than under value-preserving action
Discuss connections to existing work in geometric vision (e.g., landmark-based shape analysis) more thoroughly

Provide more substantial real data comparisons with ground truth or more convincing evidence of superiority over warpMix

The justification for choosing norm-preserving over value-preserving actions is insufficient and potentially flawed
The novelty relative to existing geometric vision work on handling equivalence classes and non-minimal parameterizations is not well articulated
The paper does not adequately address how to optimally select which element of an equivalence class to use, or discuss alternatives
Real data experiments lack ground truth, making it difficult to assess whether the proposed method truly outperforms warpMix
The motivation for introducing phase variability and its necessity is not clearly explained in the introduction

The counterargument that value-preserving action also allows finding equivalent f for any g and γ undermines the justification
Footnote 1 reference to Example 6.5.2 in Bogachev (1998) is cited but not sufficiently rigorous
Line 119 contains a potential typo: 'gamma in Gamma?' suggesting unclear notation
The choice of constraint at line 76 is presented without discussion of optimality or alternatives
MCMC sampling approach is used but no comparison provided with optimization-based alternatives
The model assumes common discretization and Gaussian errors, which may not be realistic for all applications

Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper presents a Bayesian approach to functional mixed models with a focus on statistical inference in high-noise regimes. While the technical execution is sound and the work demonstrates strong empirical performance on synthetic datasets, significant concerns exist regarding its suitability for the NeurIPS venue and its relevance to the broader machine learning community. The paper's accessibility is substantially hindered by advanced mathematical notation and, more critically, the fundamental motivations for why machine learning practitioners should engage with this work remain unclear.

## Strengths

The paper demonstrates technical rigor in its methodology. The strong modeling assumptions underlying the approach enable recovery of true signals in high-noise scenarios where conventional methods such as Gaussian Processes struggle—a notable achievement demonstrated on the synthetic benchmarks. Additionally, the application to the Berkeley growth spurt dataset illustrates potential utility for real-world functional data analysis. The introduction is well-articulated and provides a reasonable entry point to the work.

## Major Weaknesses

**Venue and Audience Mismatch**: The most significant concern is whether this work belongs at NeurIPS. The paper reads more as a specialized statistics contribution than as a machine learning advance. The content, methodology, and focus on functional mixed models with Bayesian inference align more naturally with a statistics-focused venue. This fundamental mismatch raises questions about whether the intended audience will find the work sufficiently relevant or impactful.

**Unclear Motivation and Relevance**: The paper fails to articulate why the machine learning community should care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian treatment. Beyond demonstrating technical competence, the work lacks clear connections to broader machine learning problems or challenges that practitioners face. The niche nature of the problem setting and the limited scope of applications presented reinforce the impression that this is a specialized statistical methodology rather than a contribution advancing the field of machine learning.

**Accessibility Barriers**: Despite technical quality, the paper is difficult to access due to its advanced mathematical framework and complex notation. The presentation creates a substantial barrier for readers unfamiliar with functional data analysis and Bayesian hierarchical modeling. This accessibility issue is particularly problematic given that the practical motivations remain obscure—readers must navigate dense mathematics without clear understanding of why they should invest the effort.

## Minor Weaknesses

The datasets considered feel niche-oriented, and the paper does not make a compelling case for why broader machine learning audiences should be interested in these specific applications. The practical impact and generalizability of the approach to other domains remain uncertain.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Clarify General Motivations**: Articulate more clearly what general machine learning problems this work addresses and why the proposed approach offers advantages beyond the specific functional data setting.

2. **Improve Accessibility**: Restructure the mathematical presentation to be more accessible. Consider providing intuitive explanations before formal definitions, and clarify notation throughout.

3. **Reconsider Venue**: Seriously consider whether a statistics journal would be a more appropriate venue. This would better align the work with its likely audience and potentially increase its impact within the relevant community.

4. **Strengthen Connections to ML**: Develop clearer connections between functional mixed models and recognized machine learning problems. Demonstrate broader applicability beyond the current narrow focus.

## Recommendation

While the paper exhibits technical competence and achieves its stated objectives on synthetic data, it does not meet the bar for acceptance at NeurIPS. The work is better positioned as a specialized statistics contribution and would benefit from submission to a more appropriate venue. The fundamental issue is not technical quality but rather relevance to the machine learning community and clarity of motivation for this specific audience.

**Rating: 5 (Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold)**

Summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper addresses the important problem of recovering the size-and-shape characteristics of fixed effect functions in functional mixed models using Bayesian inference. While the work makes a meaningful contribution to this challenging area and provides solid theoretical foundations, there are significant concerns regarding the generalizability of the proposed approach, its computational practicality, and its comparative advantages over existing alternatives.

## Strengths

1. **Relevant Problem**: The paper tackles a genuinely difficult and underexplored problem in functional data analysis—reliably recovering unknown functional forms through Bayesian sampling in a mixed effects framework. This is a worthwhile research direction.

2. **Theoretical Contributions**: The derivation of the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio is mathematically sound and represents a non-trivial theoretical contribution. The analytical framework is well-developed.

3. **Convergence Analysis**: The authors demonstrate convergence of their method through numerical experiments, providing empirical validation of the proposed approach.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Model Generalizability and Flexibility**: A critical concern is whether the fixed model specification in Equation (3) can genuinely recover arbitrary functions without requiring function-specific prior adjustments. The paper suggests that different prior models may need to be tailored for different functional forms, which substantially undermines the claimed generality of the approach. This limitation requires explicit discussion and clarification.

2. **Insufficient Computational Efficiency Analysis**: The paper provides minimal discussion of running time and computational cost. For a Bayesian sampling-based method, computational efficiency is paramount. The authors should report actual wall-clock times, computational complexity analysis, and scalability properties, particularly as the dimensionality of the functional data increases.

3. **Parameter Sensitivity Analysis**: The sensitivity of the method to the choice of θ (theta) and related hyperparameters is not adequately explored. This is a critical omission, as practitioners need guidance on how to set these parameters and how sensitive results are to these choices.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

1. **Comparative Advantages**: The paper does not adequately position itself relative to alternative approaches, particularly modern neural network-based methods for function recovery. What concrete advantages does the Bayesian approach offer over these alternatives?

2. **Model Flexibility**: The extent to which a single fixed model can handle diverse function types—smooth functions, functions with discontinuities, periodic functions, etc.—remains unclear. More discussion of the model's scope and limitations would be valuable.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Conduct and report comprehensive parameter sensitivity analysis for θ and other key hyperparameters. This should include visualizations showing how results vary across reasonable parameter ranges.

2. Provide detailed computational efficiency evaluation, including:
   - Actual running times for the proposed method on problems of varying complexity
   - Computational complexity analysis
   - Comparison with alternative approaches where feasible
   - Discussion of scalability properties

3. Explicitly address how the model in Equation (3) achieves flexibility in recovering different functional forms. Clarify whether function-specific prior specifications are necessary and, if so, provide principled guidance for practitioners.

4. Discuss the relationship between the fixed model structure and the ability to recover arbitrary functions, potentially with illustrative examples demonstrating both successes and limitations.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid contribution to an important problem in functional data analysis, with sound theoretical development. However, the work suffers from significant limitations that prevent it from being a strong contribution. The generalizability of the approach remains questionable, the computational efficiency is insufficiently characterized, and the practical advantages over alternative methods are unclear. These concerns must be addressed to strengthen the work's impact and utility for practitioners.

The paper would benefit from substantial revision addressing the computational and flexibility concerns before publication.

---

**Decision: 5 (Borderline)**

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:

2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

Questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

Rating:
5

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Bayesian Functional Mixed Effects Model for Size-and-Shape Inference

## Summary

This paper introduces a Bayesian functional mixed effects model that infers the size-and-shape of fixed effects through norm-preserving actions of phase functions. The authors propose treating size-and-shape as a geometric constraint distinct from traditional shape analysis approaches. While the methodological contribution is interesting and the presentation is generally clear, the work suffers from several significant limitations that prevent it from making a sufficiently strong contribution for acceptance. These include unclear hyperparameter selection methodology, limited evaluation scope, notation inconsistencies, and insufficient discussion of practical motivation and failure modes.

## Strengths

The paper presents a genuinely novel contribution to functional mixed effects modeling. The introduction of unrestricted phase variation within a Bayesian framework is a meaningful advance over existing approaches, and the geometric perspective on size-and-shape inference offers a fresh viewpoint distinct from conventional shape analysis methods. The paper is generally well-written and concise, with most formulations explained adequately. The presentation flows logically through the methodological development, making it accessible to readers familiar with functional data analysis.

## Major Weaknesses

**1. Hyperparameter Selection and Sensitivity Analysis**

The most critical issue is the unclear methodology for selecting the basis function ranks Bf and Br. The paper does not explicitly state whether these hyperparameters are chosen manually or through an automated procedure, nor does it provide any sensitivity analysis despite these being fundamental to model specification. This is particularly problematic given that the approach inherently fixes basis ranks a priori, similar to earlier LDA-style formulations, which limits the model's flexibility. The authors should clarify the selection procedure and demonstrate robustness through comprehensive sensitivity analysis.

**2. Limited and Inconsistent Evaluation**

The experimental evaluation is insufficient in scope. The synthetic experiments employ n=100,000 samples while real data experiments use only n=93—a stark inconsistency that fails to test the method's performance in realistic limited-data scenarios. Comparison is restricted to a single baseline method (warpMix), which is inadequate for establishing the method's relative merits. The paper would benefit from evaluation on multiple datasets with several competing approaches. Additionally, no computational cost analysis is provided, leaving unclear the accuracy-versus-efficiency trade-offs compared to existing methods.

**3. Notation and Technical Clarity**

Notation inconsistencies throughout the paper impede readability. Vectors, matrices, and scalars are not consistently distinguished, and dimensionality information is frequently omitted. The transpose operator, for instance, is represented inconsistently. These issues, while not fundamental, suggest insufficient attention to presentation rigor and make verification of technical claims more difficult.

**4. Insufficient Justification of Design Choices**

The selection of B-spline basis functions for random effects is not justified against other piecewise polynomial alternatives available in the literature. Similarly, the choice of modified Fourier basis for the fixed effect function lacks explicit motivation. The paper would strengthen its contribution by justifying these architectural decisions or exploring whether basis functions could be learned jointly with weight coefficients rather than fixed a priori.

## Minor Weaknesses

- **Missing practical motivation**: The paper does not articulate compelling real-world applications or scenarios where this approach would be particularly valuable compared to existing methods.
- **Incomplete referencing**: Figures 1(b) and (c) are presented but not cited in the introduction.
- **No failure case analysis**: The paper lacks discussion of scenarios where the method performs poorly or fails, and the limitations section is not adequately analyzed.
- **Limited prior exploration**: While two prior models on phase functions (PM1 and PM2) are proposed, evaluation does not explore alternative specifications or justify the chosen priors.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Clarify whether Bf and Br can be inferred automatically rather than fixed a priori, and if not, provide principled methodology for their selection.
2. Conduct thorough sensitivity analysis demonstrating robustness to hyperparameter choices.
3. Redesign experiments to use consistent sample sizes across synthetic and real data, with explicit testing on limited-data scenarios.
4. Expand evaluation to include multiple datasets and at least 2-3 additional baseline methods beyond warpMix.
5. Provide computational cost comparisons between the proposed method and existing approaches.
6. Justify the choice of B-spline basis and modified Fourier basis, or explore learned basis functions.
7. Add practical motivations and real-world applications in the introduction.
8. Correct notation inconsistencies and explicitly specify dimensionality throughout.
9. Discuss failure cases and provide a thorough limitations analysis.
10. Cite Figures 1(b) and (c) appropriately in the introduction.

## Overall Assessment

The paper makes a novel methodological contribution to Bayesian functional mixed effects modeling with a geometric perspective on size-and-shape inference. However, it falls short of the standards expected for acceptance due to unclear hyperparameter selection methodology, limited experimental evaluation, notation inconsistencies, and insufficient discussion of practical relevance and limitations. The work would benefit substantially from addressing the hyperparameter selection issue, expanding the experimental evaluation, and improving technical clarity before resubmission.

**Recommendation: Reject (5)**

The paper requires major revisions to the experimental evaluation, hyperparameter methodology, and presentation before it can be considered for publication at a top-tier venue.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Chain-of-Preference Optimization for Improved LLM Reasoning

## Summary

This paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a method that enhances large language model reasoning capabilities by leveraging preference data derived from Tree-of-Thought (ToT) approaches. The key contribution is demonstrating that reasoning performance can be improved without incurring the computational overhead typically associated with ToT. While the work presents an interesting idea and is generally well-executed, there are notable limitations in evaluation scope, analytical depth, and generalizability that prevent it from making a strong contribution to the field.

## Strengths

The paper's core contribution is conceptually sound and novel. The insight that preference data from ToT can be distilled into a more efficient training signal is valuable and well-motivated. The authors effectively demonstrate that CPO achieves performance improvements comparable to ToT while maintaining the low inference latency of standard chain-of-thought approaches—an important practical advantage.

The writing quality is commendable; the paper is clearly structured and easy to follow, making the methodology and results accessible to readers. This clarity is particularly valuable when presenting a technique that bridges multiple existing approaches.

## Major Weaknesses

**Limited Evaluation Scope**: The experimental evaluation is restricted to relatively simple reasoning benchmarks. The absence of evaluation on more challenging tasks such as MATH significantly limits claims about the method's broader applicability. These simpler benchmarks may not adequately stress-test the approach or reveal its limitations on genuinely complex reasoning problems.

**Unexamined Diversity Reduction**: A critical concern is that fine-tuning on ToT-generated reasoning paths may reduce the diversity of outputs produced by the model. This has not been systematically investigated. Since diversity in reasoning paths is often beneficial for exploration and robustness, this potential negative effect deserves thorough analysis. The paper should quantitatively measure how path diversity changes before and after CPO fine-tuning.

**Dependency on ToT Quality**: The method's effectiveness is fundamentally constrained by the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If the base ToT approach produces suboptimal or limited reasoning paths, the preference data used for fine-tuning will inherit these limitations. The paper does not adequately discuss this dependency or propose strategies to mitigate the impact of poor-quality preference data.

**Limited Model Coverage**: Evaluation is restricted to older or smaller language models. The applicability to more advanced and contemporary models such as LLaMA3 remains unclear. Given the rapid evolution of the field, demonstrating effectiveness on state-of-the-art models would substantially strengthen the contribution.

**Insufficient Interpretability Analysis**: The paper lacks deep investigation into why certain reasoning paths are preferred by CPO. This absence of interpretability analysis limits our understanding of the method's mechanisms and reduces confidence in the optimized reasoning decisions. Qualitative analysis examining which path characteristics are rewarded by the preference optimization would provide valuable insights.

## Minor Weaknesses

The technical presentation could be strengthened by analyzing how specific characteristics of reasoning paths change following CPO fine-tuning. Understanding these shifts would illuminate the optimization process and its effects on model behavior.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Expand the experimental evaluation to include more challenging benchmarks, particularly MATH and similar complex reasoning tasks, to demonstrate broader applicability.

2. Conduct a systematic investigation of reasoning path diversity before and after CPO fine-tuning, using appropriate diversity metrics and statistical analysis.

3. Provide explicit analysis of how ToT path quality affects CPO performance, and discuss potential mitigation strategies when ToT produces suboptimal reasoning paths.

4. Extend experiments to advanced language models (e.g., LLaMA3) to establish relevance to current state-of-the-art systems.

5. Include qualitative analysis and interpretability studies examining why certain reasoning paths are preferred, potentially through attention visualization, path characteristic analysis, or other interpretability techniques.

## Overall Assessment

CPO presents an interesting and practical approach to improving LLM reasoning without sacrificing inference efficiency. The core idea is sound and the paper is well-written. However, the work suffers from significant limitations that prevent it from making a sufficiently strong contribution. The evaluation is too narrow, critical dependencies are not adequately explored, and the lack of interpretability analysis leaves important questions unanswered. The method's applicability to contemporary models and its behavior on genuinely challenging tasks remain unclear.

With substantial revisions addressing the evaluation scope, diversity analysis, and interpretability concerns, this work could make a solid contribution. In its current form, however, it falls short of the standards expected for a strong acceptance.

**Recommendation: Borderline Accept (6/10)**

The paper merits publication given its novel approach and practical benefits, but acceptance is contingent on the understanding that significant limitations exist. Authors are encouraged to address the weaknesses outlined above in future work to strengthen the contribution and broaden its impact.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.
reviews.strengths: The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.
reviews.weaknesses: The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.

The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.
