reviews.summary: This paper proposes an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and simulation model. Their method assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and it uses neural samplers to approximate the prior and a variational objective that encourages proximity of both the marginal likelihood to the true data distribution and the estimated source distribution to some known prior distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularisation). In particular, proximity to the true data distribution is measured with a sliced Wasserstein distance, due to its fast computation and differentiability (preserving the differentiable pipeline). The authors present experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model.
reviews.strengths:
*Originality*

The authors consider using a sample based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is useful since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions. 

*Quality* 

The experimental section presents good and extensive empirical testing on a number of toy benchmark models, in addition to two additional more complex simulation models. Their approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1.

*Clarity*

The clarity of writing is generally good. There are a few minor errors in the writing that made it not absolutely clear all the way through:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 isn't a full sentence (or, if it is, then it not well-written because I have read it multiple times and could not turn it into a full sentence in my head).
- There is a subscript $\phi$ missing in Equation 4. 

*Significance*

I think this paper will be of some interest to the community and the techniques presented used by practitioners.
reviews.weaknesses: My main concern is that the work presented looks like it is not a large or significant contribution beyond what is already present in the literature. From my perspective, it looks like someone could reasonably argue that the contribution is just to use a different sample-based loss function to capture the discrepancy between the data distribution and simulator's marginal likelihood. It also isn't quite clear to me how exactly the approach that uses the surrogate model (Sur. in Table 1) is different from what is presented in Vandegar et al. (2020).
reviews.questions:
Could the authors provide some more detail and clarification on how their approach differs/relates to/builds on previous works please? Ideally at least those papers already cited such as Vandegar et al. (2020), but I think some additional related literature is also missing, such as references [1] (relevant since it discusses variational approaches to targeting generalised Bayesian posteriors) and [2] (relevant to the problem of source estimation in complex simulation models) below. Is there anything that we lose by using something like the Sliced Wasserstein Distance instead of a likelihood-based discrepancy?

[1] _Knoblauch, J., Jewson, J., & Damoulas, T. (2022). An optimization-centric view on Bayes' rule: Reviewing and generalizing variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 23(132), 1-109._

[2] _Dyer, J., Quera-Bofarull, A., Bishop, N., Farmer, J. D., Calinescu, A., & Wooldridge, M. (2024, May). Population Synthesis as Scenario Generation for Simulation-based Planning under Uncertainty. In Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (pp. 490-498)._
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide a good discussion of the limitations towards the end of the paper. Perhaps something additional to discuss would be limitations associated with simulators that involve discrete randomness, which (as far as I can tell) aren't tested in this paper but which often appears in simulation modelling and presents an additional complication when the requirement is that sampling from the simulator is a differentiable operation (see e.g. [1] below). I also do not see any problems related to the broader social impact of this work. 

[1] _Arya, G., Schauer, M., Schäfer, F., & Rackauckas, C. (2022). Automatic differentiation of programs with discrete randomness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 10435-10447._
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and simulation model. Their method assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and it uses neural samplers to approximate the prior and a variational objective that encourages proximity of both the marginal likelihood to the true data distribution and the estimated source distribution to some known prior distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularisation). In particular, proximity to the true data distribution is measured with a sliced Wasserstein distance, due to its fast computation and differentiability (preserving the differentiable pipeline). The authors present experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model.

Strengths:
*Originality*

The authors consider using a sample based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is useful since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions. 

*Quality* 

The experimental section presents good and extensive empirical testing on a number of toy benchmark models, in addition to two additional more complex simulation models. Their approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1.

*Clarity*

The clarity of writing is generally good. There are a few minor errors in the writing that made it not absolutely clear all the way through:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 isn't a full sentence (or, if it is, then it not well-written because I have read it multiple times and could not turn it into a full sentence in my head).
- There is a subscript $\phi$ missing in Equation 4. 

*Significance*

I think this paper will be of some interest to the community and the techniques presented used by practitioners.

Weaknesses:
My main concern is that the work presented looks like it is not a large or significant contribution beyond what is already present in the literature. From my perspective, it looks like someone could reasonably argue that the contribution is just to use a different sample-based loss function to capture the discrepancy between the data distribution and simulator's marginal likelihood. It also isn't quite clear to me how exactly the approach that uses the surrogate model (Sur. in Table 1) is different from what is presented in Vandegar et al. (2020).

Questions:
Could the authors provide some more detail and clarification on how their approach differs/relates to/builds on previous works please? Ideally at least those papers already cited such as Vandegar et al. (2020), but I think some additional related literature is also missing, such as references [1] (relevant since it discusses variational approaches to targeting generalised Bayesian posteriors) and [2] (relevant to the problem of source estimation in complex simulation models) below. Is there anything that we lose by using something like the Sliced Wasserstein Distance instead of a likelihood-based discrepancy?

[1] _Knoblauch, J., Jewson, J., & Damoulas, T. (2022). An optimization-centric view on Bayes' rule: Reviewing and generalizing variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 23(132), 1-109._

[2] _Dyer, J., Quera-Bofarull, A., Bishop, N., Farmer, J. D., Calinescu, A., & Wooldridge, M. (2024, May). Population Synthesis as Scenario Generation for Simulation-based Planning under Uncertainty. In Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (pp. 490-498)._

Limitations:
The authors provide a good discussion of the limitations towards the end of the paper. Perhaps something additional to discuss would be limitations associated with simulators that involve discrete randomness, which (as far as I can tell) aren't tested in this paper but which often appears in simulation modelling and presents an additional complication when the requirement is that sampling from the simulator is a differentiable operation (see e.g. [1] below). I also do not see any problems related to the broader social impact of this work. 

[1] _Arya, G., Schauer, M., Schäfer, F., & Rackauckas, C. (2022). Automatic differentiation of programs with discrete randomness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 10435-10447._

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Maximum-Entropy Source Distribution Estimation via Sample-Based Losses

## Summary

This paper proposes a method for estimating source distributions in simulation-based inference by maximizing entropy subject to a discrepancy constraint measured via Sliced-Wasserstein distance. Rather than relying on likelihood-based approaches, the method employs sample-based loss functions to handle simulators without tractable likelihoods. While the work is technically sound and well-presented with solid experimental validation, significant concerns remain regarding the novelty and contribution beyond existing approaches, particularly in relation to recent work by Vandegar et al. (2020).

## Strengths

The paper addresses a practically important problem and demonstrates several notable strengths:

1. **Principled approach to intractable likelihoods**: The use of sample-based discrepancy measures (Sliced-Wasserstein distance) elegantly sidesteps the requirement for tractable likelihoods, making the method applicable to a broad class of complex simulators.

2. **Solid theoretical foundation**: Proposition 2.1 provides valuable theoretical grounding by establishing the uniqueness of maximum entropy source distributions, giving the approach a principled basis.

3. **Comprehensive experimental evaluation**: The paper demonstrates applicability across diverse settings—from toy problems to high-dimensional simulators and real neuroscience data (including the Hodgkin-Huxley model)—showing practical utility.

4. **Clear presentation**: The paper is generally well-written with good clarity in exposition and logical organization.

5. **Computational efficiency**: The choice of Sliced-Wasserstein distance (O(n log n)) over full Wasserstein distance (O(n³)) is well-motivated from a practical standpoint.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, the paper has significant limitations that constrain its contribution:

1. **Questionable novelty**: The core contribution appears to be primarily a substitution of the distance metric—replacing likelihood-based discrepancy measures with Sliced-Wasserstein distance—rather than a fundamentally new methodological advance. This raises concerns about whether the work constitutes a sufficient contribution beyond existing methods.

2. **Insufficient differentiation from prior work**: The relationship between this work and Vandegar et al. (2020) requires substantial clarification. In particular, the paper does not adequately explain how the surrogate model approach (denoted "Sur." in Table 1) differs from what was already presented in the prior work, nor does it clearly articulate what is genuinely novel in this contribution.

3. **Limited engagement with related literature**: The paper lacks discussion of relevant recent work on variational approaches to generalized Bayesian posteriors and related methods for source estimation in complex simulation models. This gap makes it difficult to properly contextualize the contribution within the broader literature.

4. **Missing trade-off analysis**: The paper does not discuss what is gained and lost by adopting Sliced-Wasserstein distance compared to likelihood-based alternatives. A thoughtful analysis of these trade-offs would strengthen the motivation and positioning of the work.

5. **Unaddressed practical limitation**: Simulators with discrete randomness are common in practice, yet the paper neither tests the method on such simulators nor discusses the limitations this imposes. This is a significant oversight given the claimed practical relevance of the approach.

## Minor Issues

1. **Typographical error**: Equation 4 appears to have a missing subscript φ.

2. **Writing quality**: The last sentence of the first paragraph in Section 1 is incomplete or poorly constructed and should be revised.

3. **Technical clarity**: While the method uses neural samplers for parameterization and the Kozachenko-Leonenko estimator for entropy estimation, and employs dynamical scheduling of the λ parameter, these choices could benefit from more explicit justification.

## Suggestions for Improvement

To address the identified weaknesses, I recommend:

1. Provide detailed, explicit comparison with Vandegar et al. (2020), clearly delineating what is novel in the present work versus what has been previously established.

2. Expand the related work section to include recent literature on variational inference, generalized Bayesian approaches, and population synthesis methods for scenario generation.

3. Include a substantive discussion of the trade-offs inherent in using Sliced-Wasserstein distance, highlighting both advantages and limitations relative to likelihood-based approaches.

4. Extend the experimental evaluation to include simulators with discrete randomness, and discuss the applicability and limitations of the method in such settings.

5. Correct the minor typographical and grammatical issues noted above.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a competent application of sample-based losses to source distribution estimation with adequate experimental validation. However, the work reads primarily as an incremental methodological adjustment rather than a substantial advance. The core issue is the insufficient novelty relative to existing approaches, combined with inadequate clarification of how this work builds upon and extends prior work. While the practical utility of the method is evident, the contribution to the field is marginal without clearer articulation of novel insights or significant methodological advances.

The paper would be strengthened considerably by more thoroughly positioning the work within the existing literature, clearly delineating novel contributions, and addressing the practical limitations that have been overlooked.

**Recommendation: Borderline (Score: 5)**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and simulation model. Their method assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and it uses neural samplers to approximate the prior and a variational objective that encourages proximity of both the marginal likelihood to the true data distribution and the estimated source distribution to some known prior distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularisation). In particular, proximity to the true data distribution is measured with a sliced Wasserstein distance, due to its fast computation and differentiability (preserving the differentiable pipeline). The authors present experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model.

Strengths:
*Originality*

The authors consider using a sample based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is useful since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions. 

*Quality* 

The experimental section presents good and extensive empirical testing on a number of toy benchmark models, in addition to two additional more complex simulation models. Their approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1.

*Clarity*

The clarity of writing is generally good. There are a few minor errors in the writing that made it not absolutely clear all the way through:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 isn't a full sentence (or, if it is, then it not well-written because I have read it multiple times and could not turn it into a full sentence in my head).
- There is a subscript $\phi$ missing in Equation 4. 

*Significance*

I think this paper will be of some interest to the community and the techniques presented used by practitioners.

Weaknesses:
My main concern is that the work presented looks like it is not a large or significant contribution beyond what is already present in the literature. From my perspective, it looks like someone could reasonably argue that the contribution is just to use a different sample-based loss function to capture the discrepancy between the data distribution and simulator's marginal likelihood. It also isn't quite clear to me how exactly the approach that uses the surrogate model (Sur. in Table 1) is different from what is presented in Vandegar et al. (2020).

Questions:
Could the authors provide some more detail and clarification on how their approach differs/relates to/builds on previous works please? Ideally at least those papers already cited such as Vandegar et al. (2020), but I think some additional related literature is also missing, such as references [1] (relevant since it discusses variational approaches to targeting generalised Bayesian posteriors) and [2] (relevant to the problem of source estimation in complex simulation models) below. Is there anything that we lose by using something like the Sliced Wasserstein Distance instead of a likelihood-based discrepancy?

[1] _Knoblauch, J., Jewson, J., & Damoulas, T. (2022). An optimization-centric view on Bayes' rule: Reviewing and generalizing variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 23(132), 1-109._

[2] _Dyer, J., Quera-Bofarull, A., Bishop, N., Farmer, J. D., Calinescu, A., & Wooldridge, M. (2024, May). Population Synthesis as Scenario Generation for Simulation-based Planning under Uncertainty. In Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (pp. 490-498)._

Limitations:
The authors provide a good discussion of the limitations towards the end of the paper. Perhaps something additional to discuss would be limitations associated with simulators that involve discrete randomness, which (as far as I can tell) aren't tested in this paper but which often appears in simulation modelling and presents an additional complication when the requirement is that sampling from the simulator is a differentiable operation (see e.g. [1] below). I also do not see any problems related to the broader social impact of this work. 

[1] _Arya, G., Schauer, M., Schäfer, F., & Rackauckas, C. (2022). Automatic differentiation of programs with discrete randomness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 10435-10447._

Rating:
5

title: Improving Deep Learning Optimization through Constrained Parameter Regularization

reviews.summary:
This paper presents Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR) as an alternative to traditional weight decay.
CPR enforces an upper bound on the L2-norm of individual parameter matrices. It frames learning as a constraint optimization problem solved with the augmented Lagrangian method and can be integrated seamlessly with gradient-based optimizers. 
During training, CPR dynamically tailors regularization and reduces the need for hyperparameter selection. 
Empirical results on computer vision and language modeling tasks demonstrate its effectiveness.
reviews.strengths:
- The proposed CPR is straightforward to implement and can be integrated with existing gradient-based optimization methods.
- Experiments demonstrate CPR’s effectiveness in various tasks compared to traditional weight decay. Results also show that the performance of CPR is robust to hyperparameter selection and needs less training budget.
- The paper is well-written and easy to understand, making the concepts accessible.
reviews.weaknesses: The general idea of using adaptive regularization does not seem very novel. However, the reviewer does not specialize in this area and won't view this as a significant flaw given CPR’s experimental results.
reviews.questions:
1. CPR constrains parameters with an L2 norm upper bound. Will this method impede the model’s learning ability under complex tasks, e.g., very slow convergence or loss of training accuracy? 
2. It would be interesting to investigate CPR’s robustness to noise and distributional shifts. How does CPR perform when the training data is noisy or comes from a different distribution than the test data?

Summary:
This paper presents Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR) as an alternative to traditional weight decay.
CPR enforces an upper bound on the L2-norm of individual parameter matrices. It frames learning as a constraint optimization problem solved with the augmented Lagrangian method and can be integrated seamlessly with gradient-based optimizers. 
During training, CPR dynamically tailors regularization and reduces the need for hyperparameter selection. 
Empirical results on computer vision and language modeling tasks demonstrate its effectiveness.

Strengths:
- The proposed CPR is straightforward to implement and can be integrated with existing gradient-based optimization methods.
- Experiments demonstrate CPR’s effectiveness in various tasks compared to traditional weight decay. Results also show that the performance of CPR is robust to hyperparameter selection and needs less training budget.
- The paper is well-written and easy to understand, making the concepts accessible.

Weaknesses:
The general idea of using adaptive regularization does not seem very novel. However, the reviewer does not specialize in this area and won't view this as a significant flaw given CPR’s experimental results.

Questions:
1. CPR constrains parameters with an L2 norm upper bound. Will this method impede the model’s learning ability under complex tasks, e.g., very slow convergence or loss of training accuracy? 
2. It would be interesting to investigate CPR’s robustness to noise and distributional shifts. How does CPR perform when the training data is noisy or comes from a different distribution than the test data?

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Constrained Parameter Regularization

## Summary

This paper introduces Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR), a novel approach to regularization that reformulates the learning problem as a constrained optimization task solved via the augmented Lagrangian method. Rather than applying a uniform penalty coefficient across all parameters, CPR enforces individual L2-norm upper bounds on parameter matrices, with separate Lagrange multipliers tailored to each layer. The authors demonstrate that this method outperforms traditional weight decay across multiple domains including computer vision and language modeling tasks.

## Strengths

The paper presents several notable strengths. First, the proposed method is remarkably straightforward to implement and can be readily integrated with any gradient-based optimizer, which enhances its practical applicability. The experimental validation is comprehensive and convincing, demonstrating clear improvements over weight decay baselines across diverse tasks. Particularly compelling is CPR's robustness to hyperparameter selection—by replacing penalty coefficients with interpretable upper bounds, the method reduces the burden of hyperparameter tuning compared to traditional approaches.

The paper is also well-written and clearly presented, making the core concepts accessible to readers. The motivation for moving away from uniform regularization toward parameter-specific constraints is intuitive and well-articulated.

## Weaknesses

The primary limitation concerns novelty. The general concept of adaptive regularization—where regularization is tailored to individual parameters rather than applied uniformly—is not particularly novel. While the authors' specific instantiation through constrained optimization and the augmented Lagrangian method provides a concrete realization of this idea, the conceptual contribution is somewhat incremental. I acknowledge that my assessment of novelty may be limited by my own expertise, and the strong empirical results do mitigate this concern to some degree.

## Major Concerns and Suggestions for Future Work

Despite the positive empirical results on the evaluated tasks, several important questions remain unanswered:

1. **Performance on Complex Tasks**: The paper would benefit from investigation into whether the L2-norm constraints imposed by CPR might impede learning on particularly complex tasks. Do the constraints cause slow convergence or prevent the model from achieving competitive training accuracy on large-scale or highly complex problems?

2. **Robustness to Noise and Distribution Shift**: The current evaluation does not adequately address CPR's robustness properties. How does the method perform when training data contains significant noise or when there is a distributional shift between training and test sets? This is an important practical consideration that deserves empirical investigation.

3. **Noise Sensitivity**: Related to the above, evaluating CPR's sensitivity to noisy training data would strengthen confidence in the method's reliability across diverse real-world scenarios.

## Minor Comments

The technical approach of using separate Lagrange multipliers for each parameter matrix to achieve individualized regularization is sound. The reduction in hyperparameter burden compared to weight decay is a genuine practical advantage, and the empirical evidence of reduced training budget requirements is noteworthy.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid empirical contribution with a simple, implementable method that demonstrates consistent improvements over weight decay. The work is technically sound and well-presented. However, the conceptual novelty is limited, and the evaluation, while broad, does not fully explore the method's limitations on complex tasks or its robustness properties. The paper represents competent work that will be of interest to practitioners, though it falls short of making a strong conceptual advance to the field.

**Recommendation**: Accept (6/10)

The combination of practical utility, clear presentation, and strong empirical results justifies acceptance, though the limited conceptual novelty and unanswered questions about robustness prevent a higher rating.

Summary:
This paper illustrates a new training algorithm to improve the weight decay strategy. Instead of giving the same strength to all weights in weight decay, the proposed method penalizes only the elements that are larger than the threshold. Based on extensive evaluation, the proposed method performs better than AdamW/SGD+weight decay.

Strengths:
1. The experiment is strong enough to support the proposed method.
2. The intuition is easy to understand; that is, instead of using the same weight decay strength for all weights, the strength should be different for different weights since the importance is not the same.

Weaknesses:
1. As mentioned in the paper, the computational cost is higher.
2. The evaluation of SGD can be improved since it is well known that the best test accuracy can usually be achieved when using SGD with weight decay on image classification tasks.

Questions:
See weakness.

7
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Constrained Parameter Regularization

## Summary

This paper presents Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR), a method that improves upon standard uniform weight decay by applying parameter-specific regularization constraints based on the importance of individual weight matrices. The work is supported by comprehensive experimental validation across multiple tasks and datasets. While the core contribution is intuitive and well-motivated, concerns regarding computational overhead and incomplete optimizer evaluation warrant careful consideration.

## Strengths

1. **Clear and Intuitive Core Contribution**: The fundamental idea is straightforward and compelling—rather than applying uniform regularization coefficients across all parameters, CPR enforces upper bounds on statistical measures (such as L2-norms) of individual parameter matrices. This parameter-specific approach is conceptually sound and easy to understand.

2. **Comprehensive Experimental Validation**: The experimental evaluation is thorough and effectively demonstrates the method's advantages. The results across multiple tasks and datasets provide convincing evidence that CPR outperforms uniform weight decay in practice, strengthening the paper's claims.

## Weaknesses

1. **Computational Overhead**: The paper acknowledges that CPR introduces increased computational cost during training compared to standard weight decay. While this limitation is mentioned, the practical implications deserve more thorough discussion. For practitioners considering adoption, understanding the magnitude of this overhead and its impact on training time would be valuable.

2. **Incomplete SGD Evaluation**: The evaluation of the SGD optimizer appears to lack sufficient depth. This is particularly concerning for image classification tasks, where SGD combined with weight decay is known to achieve competitive or superior test accuracy compared to adaptive methods. The current SGD experiments do not appear to be fully optimized or carefully tuned for fair comparison with the proposed method. This gap weakens the comprehensiveness of the empirical evaluation.

## Suggestions for Improvement

- **Enhanced SGD Comparison**: The authors should conduct more thorough experiments with well-tuned SGD+weight decay baselines, especially on image classification benchmarks. This should include careful hyperparameter tuning to ensure fair comparison and to better establish where CPR provides advantages over this strong baseline.

## Overall Assessment

The paper makes a solid contribution by proposing a parameter-specific regularization approach that demonstrates clear improvements over uniform weight decay. The experimental work is generally comprehensive and the method is intuitive. However, the computational cost implications and the incomplete evaluation against well-optimized SGD baselines are notable limitations. With additional attention to SGD evaluation and a more thorough discussion of computational trade-offs, this work would be strengthened. Nevertheless, the core contribution is valuable and the existing experimental evidence provides adequate support for the proposed method's effectiveness in most settings.

**Recommendation**: Accept (7/10)

The paper merits publication based on its clear contribution, intuitive approach, and strong experimental support, though addressing the SGD evaluation concerns would enhance its impact.

reviews.summary: The authors propose a form of regularization which adjusts the regularization strength based on the weights being inside or outside of a certain norm bound. A violation of the bound results in an increasing penalty and coefficient, while conforming to the bound results in a decreasing or zero regularization penalty.
reviews.strengths:
- The method proposes a way to adaptively control the strength of regularization for weigth groups within a model
- The model intuitively makes sense, as the regularization term is meant to constrain weights to within a certain region. However, current optimizers leave the regularization in place regardless of how well the weights are conforming to it.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The statement on L123-124 is hard to accept, and I think it needs more explanation. Why if $F(x)$ not suitable for gradient based optimization? It seems that if $F$ and $c$ are both differentiable, then it indeed would provide iseful information to restore the feasibility of an infeasible $x$. Am I missing something here?

- It would be interesting to know what effect the training has on the rubustness to OOD inputs or noise perturbed inputs. Is it possible to run some tests on the trained models related to CIFAR100-C and AdvSQUAD or AdvGLUE (or other suitable language dataset to measure robustness)? I ask this because I suspect that the smaller weight norm maintained throughout training would have the effect of not being overly reliant on particular weights in the model, which would likely result in a model which is more robust to OOD and adversarial inputs.

- The minor runtime overhead mentioned in the paper is actually on the order of 5-6%. The definition of minor is subjective, and I would not consider a 5% increase in runtime minor. I think it would be better to state the actual runtime increase within the main text of the paper to give the reader a better idea of the cost. 

## Minor

- L70: pertaining --> pretraining
reviews.questions: Can you add the OOD or adversarial tests mentioned above?
reviews.limitations: The limitations are discussed
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a form of regularization which adjusts the regularization strength based on the weights being inside or outside of a certain norm bound. A violation of the bound results in an increasing penalty and coefficient, while conforming to the bound results in a decreasing or zero regularization penalty.

Strengths:
- The method proposes a way to adaptively control the strength of regularization for weigth groups within a model
- The model intuitively makes sense, as the regularization term is meant to constrain weights to within a certain region. However, current optimizers leave the regularization in place regardless of how well the weights are conforming to it.

Weaknesses:
- The statement on L123-124 is hard to accept, and I think it needs more explanation. Why if $F(x)$ not suitable for gradient based optimization? It seems that if $F$ and $c$ are both differentiable, then it indeed would provide iseful information to restore the feasibility of an infeasible $x$. Am I missing something here?

- It would be interesting to know what effect the training has on the rubustness to OOD inputs or noise perturbed inputs. Is it possible to run some tests on the trained models related to CIFAR100-C and AdvSQUAD or AdvGLUE (or other suitable language dataset to measure robustness)? I ask this because I suspect that the smaller weight norm maintained throughout training would have the effect of not being overly reliant on particular weights in the model, which would likely result in a model which is more robust to OOD and adversarial inputs.

- The minor runtime overhead mentioned in the paper is actually on the order of 5-6%. The definition of minor is subjective, and I would not consider a 5% increase in runtime minor. I think it would be better to state the actual runtime increase within the main text of the paper to give the reader a better idea of the cost. 

## Minor

- L70: pertaining --> pretraining

Questions:
Can you add the OOD or adversarial tests mentioned above?

Limitations:
The limitations are discussed

Rating:
7

# Peer Review: Adaptive Regularization Control via Constrained Parameter Regularization

## Summary

This paper proposes Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR), a method for adaptively controlling regularization strength across weight groups in neural networks. The core idea is intuitive: regularization pressure should be applied selectively to weights that violate constraints rather than uniformly across all parameters. While the approach shows promise and demonstrates practical benefits, the paper has several limitations that prevent it from being a strong contribution in its current form.

## Strengths

The paper addresses a relevant problem in neural network training. The proposed adaptive regularization scheme is conceptually sound—the observation that uniform regularization may be inefficient when some weight groups already satisfy constraints is reasonable and well-motivated. The method's ability to dynamically adjust regularization strength based on constraint violations is an intuitive improvement over fixed regularization approaches. The experimental results demonstrate that CPR achieves competitive performance across multiple datasets and architectures, suggesting practical utility.

## Major Weaknesses

**1. Theoretical Justification Requires Clarification**

The explanation on lines 123-124 regarding why F(x) = max_{λ≥0}[f(x) + λ·c(x)] is unsuitable for gradient-based optimization is insufficiently justified. The authors claim this formulation is non-differentiable, but the argument needs strengthening. If both f(x) and c(x) are differentiable functions, one would expect the max operation to provide meaningful gradient information that could guide the optimization process toward feasible solutions. The paper should provide a more rigorous explanation of the mathematical or practical reasons why this formulation fails, or alternatively, discuss the conditions under which it becomes problematic. This is a critical point that affects the motivation for the proposed approach.

**2. Missing Robustness Evaluation**

A significant gap in the experimental evaluation is the absence of out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness testing. Since CPR maintains smaller weight norms throughout training compared to unconstrained training, it is reasonable to hypothesize that this could improve model robustness to distribution shifts and adversarial perturbations. However, this is never empirically tested. The paper would be substantially strengthened by evaluating robustness on benchmarks such as CIFAR100-C (for natural corruptions), AdvSQUAD, or AdvGLUE (for adversarial robustness). This evaluation would either validate an important claimed benefit or clarify the limitations of the approach.

**3. Runtime Overhead Understated**

The paper characterizes a 5-6% runtime overhead as "minor," but this assessment is subjective and potentially misleading. For practitioners, a 5-6% increase in training time is non-negligible, especially when scaled to large models or long training schedules. More importantly, this overhead is relegated to the appendix rather than being prominently disclosed in the main text. Readers deserve transparent information about computational costs upfront. The actual runtime increase should be explicitly stated in the main paper to allow informed decision-making about whether the performance gains justify the computational expense.

## Minor Issues

- **Line 70**: There is a typo where "pertaining" should read "pretraining."

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Provide a more detailed mathematical or intuitive explanation of why the max-over-λ formulation of F(x) is non-differentiable or unsuitable for gradient-based optimization. Consider discussing the conditions under which this becomes problematic.

2. Conduct comprehensive robustness evaluations using OOD benchmarks (CIFAR100-C, AdvSQUAD, AdvGLUE, etc.) to assess whether maintaining constrained weight norms improves robustness to distribution shifts and adversarial inputs.

3. Move the runtime overhead disclosure to the main paper text and present it in a balanced way that acknowledges the computational cost rather than characterizing it as negligible.

4. Correct the typo on line 70.

## Overall Assessment

CPR presents a reasonable approach to adaptive regularization that is intuitive and shows practical benefits. However, the paper has notable limitations in theoretical justification, experimental scope, and transparency regarding computational costs. The theoretical explanation for the design choice needs strengthening, the evaluation would benefit from robustness testing, and the runtime overhead should be more prominently and honestly reported. With revisions addressing these concerns, this work could make a solid contribution to the field. In its current form, while acceptable, it falls short of being a strong contribution due to incomplete justification and evaluation.

**Recommendation**: Accept with revisions (Rating: 7/10)

7

# Peer Review: Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR)

## Summary

This paper proposes Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR), a novel regularization technique that dynamically adapts regularization strength to individual parameters through constraint optimization. The work presents a technically sound approach with clear mathematical exposition and demonstrates its effectiveness across multiple deep learning domains. However, the paper has notable limitations in theoretical rigor, experimental scope, and computational cost analysis that prevent it from making a sufficiently strong contribution.

## Strengths

1. **Clear Mathematical Exposition**: The paper provides a well-organized formal description of the background and derivation process. The mathematical development is straightforward and accessible, making it easy to follow the authors' reasoning and understand the proposed method.

2. **Comprehensive Connection to Existing Methods**: The authors effectively explain the theoretical differences and connections between CPR and weight decay algorithms, situating their work within the broader landscape of optimization methods. This contextual analysis strengthens the motivation for the proposed approach.

3. **Multiple Initialization Strategies**: The design of four distinct initialization methods for the constraint upper bound κ (Kappa-K, Kappa-kI0, Kappa-WS, and Kappa-IP) demonstrates thoughtful engineering and provides practitioners with multiple options for deployment.

4. **Broad Experimental Validation**: The paper includes extensive experiments across various deep learning domains, and the results consistently verify that the proposed method is technically correct and produces improvements over baseline approaches.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Insufficient Theoretical Analysis**: The optimization algorithm lacks rigorous convergence analysis and theoretical guarantees. The paper would significantly benefit from formal proofs of convergence properties, optimality conditions, and other theoretical characterizations of the algorithm's behavior. The current presentation relies too heavily on intuition and empirical validation rather than mathematical rigor.

2. **Lack of Theoretical Justification for Initialization**: While the paper proposes four initialization strategies for κ, it provides limited theoretical support for why these particular designs should work well. The initialization algorithm appears to be primarily empirically motivated without principled theoretical foundations.

3. **Limited Experimental Scope**: The experimental evaluation relies heavily on CIFAR100, which is too small a dataset to convincingly demonstrate the superiority of a training method in modern deep learning. To establish the practical value of the proposed regularization technique, the authors must demonstrate effectiveness on larger, more challenging datasets such as ImageNet or comparable benchmarks.

4. **Incomplete Computational Cost Analysis**: The method introduces additional learnable parameters (λ and κ) compared to standard weight decay, but the paper does not adequately report the associated computational and memory overhead. While a brief mention of 0.4%-5.8% runtime increase for GPT2 is provided, this analysis is neither comprehensive nor consistent across all experimental settings, leaving uncertainty about the practical cost-benefit tradeoff.

## Minor Issues and Suggestions

- Provide formal convergence analysis with rigorous mathematical language, including convergence rates and conditions under which convergence is guaranteed.
- Develop theoretical justification for the initialization algorithm design, explaining why each strategy is expected to be effective from first principles.
- Expand the experimental evaluation to larger datasets such as ImageNet to provide more convincing evidence of the method's superiority for practical deep learning applications.
- Include detailed reporting of additional computational and memory costs across all experimental configurations, enabling practitioners to make informed decisions about adoption.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically correct regularization method with clear presentation and broad experimental validation. The core idea of parameter-specific regularization through constraint optimization is sound, and the multiple initialization strategies show careful consideration of practical implementation. However, the work falls short of making a sufficiently strong contribution due to three critical limitations: (1) the lack of theoretical convergence analysis undermines confidence in the method's reliability, (2) the initialization strategies lack theoretical grounding, and (3) the experimental evaluation on relatively small datasets combined with incomplete computational cost reporting leaves questions about practical utility unanswered.

To strengthen this work, the authors should prioritize providing formal theoretical analysis of the optimization algorithm, developing principled justifications for the initialization methods, and conducting experiments on larger-scale benchmarks with comprehensive cost analysis. These additions would transform this from a technically competent paper into a more compelling contribution to the field.

**Recommendation**: The paper makes a reasonable contribution but requires significant revisions to address the theoretical gaps and experimental limitations before it can be accepted.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a new regularization technique, Constrained Parameter Regression (CPR), to replace weight decay for training deep learning models. Conventional weight decay penalizes significant weight deviation. However, this can be too restrictive since some layers may need a larger deviation. Instead of applying the same regularization strength for all layers as in weight decay, the paper uses different hyperparameters for each layer, resulting in a more flexible regularization strength. Intuitively, a model with a larger weight $||\theta||$ is allowed more deviation under CPR. The method is theoretically motivated by the augmented Lagrangian method and tested on multiple tasks and benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
* The method is principledly motivated and intuitively explained. Overall, the paper is very well-written.

* The paper proposes multiple alternatives for setting the method's hyper-parameters, taking into account efficiency and tuning flexibility. 

* The paper demonstrates superior performance on multiple tasks with modern deep learning models, including large language models.
reviews.weaknesses:
* **The regularization strength (upper bound $k$) relies on empirical observations and intuition**. While the overall method is theoretically motivated, the upper bound $k$ setting relies mainly on intuition. Specifically, the paper does not explain why $k\leftarrow R(\theta)$ is a good strategy. Intuitively, this strategy indicates that a larger weight $||\theta||$ should have less regularization. 

* **Computer vision tasks are limited**. For classification, computer vision experiments are conducted on relatively small-scale datasets, such as CIFAR100. The paper should consider using moderate-to-large-scale datasets with well-established benchmarks, such as ImageNet, to make the empirical evidence more convincing. For segmentation, popular benchmarks, such as MSCOCO, could be a better choice to establish the superiority of this regularization.
reviews.questions:
* Why does the inflection point of the regularization function $\Delta\Delta R$ reflect a saturation of performance? Shouldn't the inflection point of the loss function $L$ be a better fit for this? 

* Could the authors report results on ImageNet? If time is constrained, fine-tuning a pre-trained model, such as CLIP's pre-trained ViT-B [1], to ImageNet is a good choice. 

* Is there any empirical evidence supporting the design choice $k\leftarrow R(\theta)$? It would be great if the authors could discuss this choice in detail. 

* Prior works in hyper-optimization [2,3] show how to optimize hyper-parameters in optimizers differetiably. This could be a valid future direction for optimizing the upper bound $k$. A new work uses hyper-optimization in an optimizer for fine-tuning [4], which shares a similar spirit to this paper at a high level. 

[1] Radford, Alec, et al. "Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021.

[2] Baydin, Atilim Gunes, et al. "Online learning rate adaptation with hypergradient descent." arXiv preprint arXiv:1703.04782 (2017).

[3] Chandra, Kartik, et al. "Gradient descent: The ultimate optimizer." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 8214-8225. 

[4] Tian, Junjiao, et al. "Fast trainable projection for robust fine-tuning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 36 (2023).
reviews.limitations: There is no potential negative societal impact.

Summary:
The paper proposes a new regularization technique, Constrained Parameter Regression (CPR), to replace weight decay for training deep learning models. Conventional weight decay penalizes significant weight deviation. However, this can be too restrictive since some layers may need a larger deviation. Instead of applying the same regularization strength for all layers as in weight decay, the paper uses different hyperparameters for each layer, resulting in a more flexible regularization strength. Intuitively, a model with a larger weight $||\theta||$ is allowed more deviation under CPR. The method is theoretically motivated by the augmented Lagrangian method and tested on multiple tasks and benchmarks.

Strengths:
* The method is principledly motivated and intuitively explained. Overall, the paper is very well-written.

* The paper proposes multiple alternatives for setting the method's hyper-parameters, taking into account efficiency and tuning flexibility. 

* The paper demonstrates superior performance on multiple tasks with modern deep learning models, including large language models.

Weaknesses:
* **The regularization strength (upper bound $k$) relies on empirical observations and intuition**. While the overall method is theoretically motivated, the upper bound $k$ setting relies mainly on intuition. Specifically, the paper does not explain why $k\leftarrow R(\theta)$ is a good strategy. Intuitively, this strategy indicates that a larger weight $||\theta||$ should have less regularization. 

* **Computer vision tasks are limited**. For classification, computer vision experiments are conducted on relatively small-scale datasets, such as CIFAR100. The paper should consider using moderate-to-large-scale datasets with well-established benchmarks, such as ImageNet, to make the empirical evidence more convincing. For segmentation, popular benchmarks, such as MSCOCO, could be a better choice to establish the superiority of this regularization.

Questions:
* Why does the inflection point of the regularization function $\Delta\Delta R$ reflect a saturation of performance? Shouldn't the inflection point of the loss function $L$ be a better fit for this? 

* Could the authors report results on ImageNet? If time is constrained, fine-tuning a pre-trained model, such as CLIP's pre-trained ViT-B [1], to ImageNet is a good choice. 

* Is there any empirical evidence supporting the design choice $k\leftarrow R(\theta)$? It would be great if the authors could discuss this choice in detail. 

* Prior works in hyper-optimization [2,3] show how to optimize hyper-parameters in optimizers differetiably. This could be a valid future direction for optimizing the upper bound $k$. A new work uses hyper-optimization in an optimizer for fine-tuning [4], which shares a similar spirit to this paper at a high level. 

[1] Radford, Alec, et al. "Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021.

[2] Baydin, Atilim Gunes, et al. "Online learning rate adaptation with hypergradient descent." arXiv preprint arXiv:1703.04782 (2017).

[3] Chandra, Kartik, et al. "Gradient descent: The ultimate optimizer." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 8214-8225. 

[4] Tian, Junjiao, et al. "Fast trainable projection for robust fine-tuning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 36 (2023).

Limitations:
There is no potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Constrained Parameter Regularization

## Summary

This paper introduces Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR), a novel regularization technique that applies layer-specific regularization strengths as an alternative to standard weight decay. The approach is grounded in the augmented Lagrangian method and demonstrates promising empirical results across multiple domains. However, while the theoretical motivation is sound and the presentation is clear, the critical design choice for setting the regularization upper bound κ lacks rigorous justification, relying instead on empirical observations and intuition.

## Strengths

The paper presents a principled approach to regularization with clear theoretical grounding in the augmented Lagrangian framework. The authors provide intuitive explanations throughout, making the methodology accessible to readers. The writing quality is consistently high, facilitating understanding of the core concepts.

A notable strength is the flexibility in hyperparameter selection, where the paper proposes multiple alternatives that balance computational efficiency with tuning flexibility. This pragmatic approach acknowledges different use cases and constraints.

Empirically, the method demonstrates superior performance across diverse applications, including modern deep learning architectures and large language models, suggesting broad applicability and practical utility.

## Major Weaknesses

The most significant limitation concerns the choice of upper bound κ. The paper sets κ ← R(θ), yet provides no principled theoretical justification for why this particular strategy should be optimal. The design choice appears motivated primarily by empirical observation rather than rigorous analysis. This is problematic because the bound selection is central to the method's operation, and without proper justification, the approach feels somewhat ad-hoc despite its theoretical motivation.

Related to this, the intuition that larger weights should receive less regularization is presented without adequate justification. Why should the magnitude of existing weights determine regularization strength? The paper would benefit from deeper analysis of this design principle.

The inflection point strategy (Kappa-IP) similarly lacks clear motivation. The criterion Δ_t Δ_t R(θ^j) < 0 is motivated by intuitions about saturation, but this is not rigorously justified. It remains unclear why the inflection point of the regularization function R should reflect performance saturation, and whether the inflection point of the loss function L might be more appropriate.

## Experimental Limitations

The computer vision experiments are restricted to relatively small-scale datasets (CIFAR100), which limits the convincingness of the results. Large-scale benchmarks like ImageNet are essential for establishing the method's effectiveness on realistic problems. Even fine-tuning pre-trained models like CLIP's ViT-B on ImageNet would substantially strengthen the empirical claims.

For segmentation tasks, the choice of medical imaging datasets is questionable when well-established benchmarks like MSCOCO would provide more convincing evidence of superiority on standard computer vision tasks. The paper would benefit from evaluation on moderate-to-large-scale datasets with established benchmarks.

## Technical Concerns

The paper provides no ablation studies or empirical analysis comparing the proposed κ ← R(θ) strategy against alternative bound-setting approaches. Why is this particular choice superior to other reasonable alternatives? Without such analysis, the design decision remains unexplained.

The relationship between the regularization strategy and the underlying optimization problem needs clearer exposition. How does this approach fundamentally address limitations of standard weight decay beyond the empirical results shown?

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Expand experimental scope**: Report ImageNet results, even if limited to fine-tuning pre-trained models. This is essential for credibility in computer vision.

2. **Provide empirical justification**: Include detailed analysis and ablation studies supporting the κ ← R(θ) design choice. Compare against alternative bound-setting strategies with clear empirical evidence.

3. **Use standard benchmarks**: For segmentation, adopt MSCOCO or similar widely-used benchmarks rather than domain-specific datasets.

4. **Strengthen theoretical foundation**: Consider connections to the hyperparameter optimization literature. Explore whether differentiable hyper-optimization methods could provide a more principled approach to setting κ, as demonstrated in prior work on optimizer hyperparameter optimization.

5. **Clarify design principles**: Provide deeper justification for why larger weights should receive less regularization and why the inflection point criterion reflects performance saturation.

## Minor Comments

The paper is generally well-written, but some sections would benefit from more rigorous exposition of the design choices. The multiple alternatives for setting hyperparameters are appreciated, though the rationale for preferring one over another could be clearer.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents an interesting and theoretically motivated approach to regularization with demonstrated practical benefits. The writing is clear and the empirical results on large language models are encouraging. However, the critical design choice for the regularization upper bound relies too heavily on empirical intuition rather than principled justification. The computer vision experiments, while showing promise, lack the scale and rigor needed to be fully convincing. The work would be substantially strengthened by: (1) providing rigorous justification or ablation studies for the κ ← R(θ) choice, (2) expanding to large-scale vision benchmarks like ImageNet, and (3) connecting more explicitly to relevant optimization literature.

Despite these limitations, the core contribution is sound and the empirical results across multiple domains suggest practical value. The paper makes a meaningful contribution to the regularization literature, though with important caveats about the theoretical justification of key design choices.

**Recommendation: Accept (5)**

The paper merits publication as it presents a novel, theoretically motivated approach with broad empirical validation. However, the authors should address the justification of key design choices and expand the experimental evaluation, particularly in computer vision, to strengthen the contribution.

Summary:
The paper proposes a new regularization technique, Constrained Parameter Regression (CPR), to replace weight decay for training deep learning models. Conventional weight decay penalizes significant weight deviation. However, this can be too restrictive since some layers may need a larger deviation. Instead of applying the same regularization strength for all layers as in weight decay, the paper uses different hyperparameters for each layer, resulting in a more flexible regularization strength. Intuitively, a model with a larger weight $||\theta||$ is allowed more deviation under CPR. The method is theoretically motivated by the augmented Lagrangian method and tested on multiple tasks and benchmarks.

Strengths:
* The method is principledly motivated and intuitively explained. Overall, the paper is very well-written.

* The paper proposes multiple alternatives for setting the method's hyper-parameters, taking into account efficiency and tuning flexibility. 

* The paper demonstrates superior performance on multiple tasks with modern deep learning models, including large language models.

Weaknesses:
* **The regularization strength (upper bound $k$) relies on empirical observations and intuition**. While the overall method is theoretically motivated, the upper bound $k$ setting relies mainly on intuition. Specifically, the paper does not explain why $k\leftarrow R(\theta)$ is a good strategy. Intuitively, this strategy indicates that a larger weight $||\theta||$ should have less regularization. 

* **Computer vision tasks are limited**. For classification, computer vision experiments are conducted on relatively small-scale datasets, such as CIFAR100. The paper should consider using moderate-to-large-scale datasets with well-established benchmarks, such as ImageNet, to make the empirical evidence more convincing. For segmentation, popular benchmarks, such as MSCOCO, could be a better choice to establish the superiority of this regularization.

Questions:
* Why does the inflection point of the regularization function $\Delta\Delta R$ reflect a saturation of performance? Shouldn't the inflection point of the loss function $L$ be a better fit for this? 

* Could the authors report results on ImageNet? If time is constrained, fine-tuning a pre-trained model, such as CLIP's pre-trained ViT-B [1], to ImageNet is a good choice. 

* Is there any empirical evidence supporting the design choice $k\leftarrow R(\theta)$? It would be great if the authors could discuss this choice in detail. 

* Prior works in hyper-optimization [2,3] show how to optimize hyper-parameters in optimizers differetiably. This could be a valid future direction for optimizing the upper bound $k$. A new work uses hyper-optimization in an optimizer for fine-tuning [4], which shares a similar spirit to this paper at a high level. 

[1] Radford, Alec, et al. "Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021.

[2] Baydin, Atilim Gunes, et al. "Online learning rate adaptation with hypergradient descent." arXiv preprint arXiv:1703.04782 (2017).

[3] Chandra, Kartik, et al. "Gradient descent: The ultimate optimizer." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 8214-8225. 

[4] Tian, Junjiao, et al. "Fast trainable projection for robust fine-tuning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 36 (2023).

Limitations:
There is no potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
5

While well-written and theoretically motivated, the approach for setting the upper bound κ relies primarily on empirical observations rather than principled justification.

title: Deep Support Vectors

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a novel Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) framework, which can be used to reconstruct data and serve as latent generative models using logits as latent. By adapting the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) condition for deep learning models, the authors introduce the DeepKKT one and show that generated DSVs using this condition exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors but apply to modern deep architectures like ConvNet and ResNet. Experiments on several common image datasets, including CIFAR10, CIFAR100, SVHN, and ImageNet, verify that DeepKKT can extract and generate DSVs. Moreover, DSVs are shown to be better than existing algorithms in few-shot dataset distillation problems.
reviews.strengths: The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Introducing deep support vectors based on an adaption of the KKT condition used in traditional support vector machines (SVM) is novel and interesting. To be honest, I did not have enough time to verify all of the mathematical verifications in the paper, and I'll leave it in other reviewers' comments on this part.  The experimental results consistently show the benefit of DVSs in several problems, such as few-shot dataset distillation and image generation.
reviews.weaknesses:
One of the proposed DSVs' most critical limitations is requiring the model to be fully pre-trained before being applied for support vector generation. This condition differs from the traditional support vector machine (SVM) framework, where the training process of the (linear) model and the support vector selection are jointly performed. Also, it is unclear how the deep model is pre-trained (e.g., with which datasets; are there any domain gap issues between the pre-training data and the main training data, etc.). Furthermore, the margin definition in Eq. (3) is unclear, and I could not understand why the larger margin is better. 

Regarding the total training loss for the DSVs generation in (9), it is unclear about the selections of the augmentation operator $\mathcal A$. Moreover, when training (9) with the traditional SGD approach, how do we guarantee that the generated deep support vectors belong to the original training data set?
reviews.questions: Please address my questions in the weaknesses section.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a novel Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) framework, which can be used to reconstruct data and serve as latent generative models using logits as latent. By adapting the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) condition for deep learning models, the authors introduce the DeepKKT one and show that generated DSVs using this condition exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors but apply to modern deep architectures like ConvNet and ResNet. Experiments on several common image datasets, including CIFAR10, CIFAR100, SVHN, and ImageNet, verify that DeepKKT can extract and generate DSVs. Moreover, DSVs are shown to be better than existing algorithms in few-shot dataset distillation problems.

Strengths:
The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Introducing deep support vectors based on an adaption of the KKT condition used in traditional support vector machines (SVM) is novel and interesting. To be honest, I did not have enough time to verify all of the mathematical verifications in the paper, and I'll leave it in other reviewers' comments on this part.  The experimental results consistently show the benefit of DVSs in several problems, such as few-shot dataset distillation and image generation.

Weaknesses:
One of the proposed DSVs' most critical limitations is requiring the model to be fully pre-trained before being applied for support vector generation. This condition differs from the traditional support vector machine (SVM) framework, where the training process of the (linear) model and the support vector selection are jointly performed. Also, it is unclear how the deep model is pre-trained (e.g., with which datasets; are there any domain gap issues between the pre-training data and the main training data, etc.). Furthermore, the margin definition in Eq. (3) is unclear, and I could not understand why the larger margin is better. 

Regarding the total training loss for the DSVs generation in (9), it is unclear about the selections of the augmentation operator $\mathcal A$. Moreover, when training (9) with the traditional SGD approach, how do we guarantee that the generated deep support vectors belong to the original training data set?

Questions:
Please address my questions in the weaknesses section.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Deep Support Vectors Framework for Deep Learning

## Summary

This paper introduces Deep Support Vectors (DSVs), a novel framework that extends classical Support Vector Machine principles through adaptation of KKT conditions to deep learning. The authors demonstrate the utility of their approach on dataset distillation and image generation tasks, achieving competitive experimental results across multiple benchmarks. While the core contribution is interesting and the paper is generally well-presented, several significant technical and methodological concerns limit its impact.

## Strengths

The paper makes a commendable contribution by bridging classical SVM theory with modern deep learning. The writing is clear and accessible, making the main ideas easy to follow. The proposed Deep Support Vectors framework is novel and conceptually interesting, offering a fresh perspective on how classical optimization principles can inform deep learning methodology.

The experimental validation is comprehensive and encouraging. The authors demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple challenging tasks including few-shot dataset distillation and image generation, with evaluations on standard benchmarks such as CIFAR10, CIFAR100, SVHN, and ImageNet. Notably, the approach generalizes well to different architectures, being successfully applied to both ConvNets and ResNets, which speaks to its broad applicability.

## Major Weaknesses

However, the paper has several critical limitations that must be addressed. The most fundamental issue is the requirement that the deep model must be fully pre-trained before support vector generation can occur. This represents a significant departure from traditional SVM methodology, where training and support vector selection are jointly optimized. The paper does not adequately justify why this sequential approach is necessary or beneficial in the deep learning setting, nor does it discuss whether joint optimization might be feasible or preferable.

Related to this, the pre-training procedure itself is insufficiently specified. The paper lacks clarity on which datasets are used for pre-training, how these are selected, and critically, whether domain gaps exist between the pre-training data and the data used for the main training procedure. These gaps directly impact the validity of the generated support vectors and deserve careful treatment.

## Technical Clarity Issues

Several key technical components require clarification. The margin definition presented in Equation (3) is not clearly explained, and the paper does not adequately justify why enforcing larger margins is beneficial for deep neural networks. This is a central concept that warrants more rigorous exposition and theoretical or empirical justification.

The formulation of the total training loss in Equation (9) involves multiple loss terms (L_stationarity, L_primal, L_tot, L_norm) whose individual roles and relative importance are unclear. Specifically, the selection criteria and design choices for the augmentation operator A are not well explained. What principles guide this selection? How sensitive is the method to different augmentation choices? These practical details are essential for reproducibility and understanding.

Additionally, there is no theoretical or empirical guarantee that the SGD optimization of Equation (9) produces vectors that belong to the original training dataset manifold. During optimization, what prevents the generated support vectors from drifting away from realistic data points? This is a non-trivial concern that should be addressed.

## Minor Issues and Suggestions

The paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of how pre-training dataset selection and potential domain shifts affect the quality and validity of the generated DSVs. This could include ablation studies or sensitivity analyses examining the impact of pre-training choices.

The authors should clarify the relationship between their approach and classical SVM theory. Specifically, they should discuss whether and how joint training of the model and support vector selection could be incorporated into the deep learning setting, or provide principled arguments for why the sequential approach is preferable.

## Missing Verification

Due to time constraints, I was unable to fully verify all mathematical derivations and ensure that the proposed conditions properly adapt KKT conditions to the deep learning setting. The authors are encouraged to provide supplementary materials with detailed proofs if not already included.

## Overall Assessment

The paper presents an interesting and novel idea with promising experimental results. The writing is clear and the experimental validation is reasonably comprehensive. However, the work suffers from insufficient technical clarity on fundamental components, inadequate justification of key design choices, and a methodological departure from classical SVM theory that lacks proper motivation. The requirement for pre-trained models and the associated domain gap concerns are particularly troubling, as they introduce potential confounds that could affect the validity of the conclusions.

With revisions addressing the technical clarity issues, the pre-training procedure, and the theoretical justification for the sequential optimization approach, this work could make a solid contribution to the field. In its current form, however, the unresolved technical and methodological concerns prevent a strong recommendation.

**Recommendation: Borderline - Weak Accept (6/10)**

The paper merits publication conditional on addressing the major concerns outlined above, particularly regarding technical clarity, pre-training procedures, and methodological justification.

Summary:
This paper introduces a novel Deep Support Vectors (DSVs) framework, which can be used to reconstruct data and serve as latent generative models using logits as latent. By adapting the traditional Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) condition for deep learning models, the authors introduce the DeepKKT one and show that generated DSVs using this condition exhibit properties similar to traditional support vectors but apply to modern deep architectures like ConvNet and ResNet. Experiments on several common image datasets, including CIFAR10, CIFAR100, SVHN, and ImageNet, verify that DeepKKT can extract and generate DSVs. Moreover, DSVs are shown to be better than existing algorithms in few-shot dataset distillation problems.

Strengths:
The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Introducing deep support vectors based on an adaption of the KKT condition used in traditional support vector machines (SVM) is novel and interesting. To be honest, I did not have enough time to verify all of the mathematical verifications in the paper, and I'll leave it in other reviewers' comments on this part.  The experimental results consistently show the benefit of DVSs in several problems, such as few-shot dataset distillation and image generation.

Weaknesses:
One of the proposed DSVs' most critical limitations is requiring the model to be fully pre-trained before being applied for support vector generation. This condition differs from the traditional support vector machine (SVM) framework, where the training process of the (linear) model and the support vector selection are jointly performed. Also, it is unclear how the deep model is pre-trained (e.g., with which datasets; are there any domain gap issues between the pre-training data and the main training data, etc.). Furthermore, the margin definition in Eq. (3) is unclear, and I could not understand why the larger margin is better. 

Regarding the total training loss for the DSVs generation in (9), it is unclear about the selections of the augmentation operator $\mathcal A$. Moreover, when training (9) with the traditional SGD approach, how do we guarantee that the generated deep support vectors belong to the original training data set?

Questions:
Please address my questions in the weaknesses section.

Rating:
6

reviews.summary:
The paper describes rethinking a classification through support vector in a way similar to SVMs. It provides additional benefits such as: 
- few-shot dataset distillation
- using the similarity of the proposed formulation to the diffusion models, the model can be transformed into a generative model
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: The paper demonstrates out-of-the-box thinking by showing that generalisation of the deep KKT conditions leads to similarity with diffusion models; the contribution of the paper is multifaceted: formulating deep KKT conditions, showing the utility of such formulation for dataset distillation, and showing how it could lead to a generative model. 
- Significance: I think this work is significant as the authors contribute towards the intersection of such important problems as dataset distillation, transparency of the ML models, and provide new insight about the connection between the KKT conditions and the diffusion models
- Clarity: the paper is clearly written, the only optional suggestion, for the ease of mathematical notation, is to use the boldface fonts for vectors, e.g., like Bishop (2006), page xi.  It also gives, in my understanding, enough reproducibility information. 
- Quality: The paper presents and sufficiently backs up the claims.
reviews.weaknesses: Clarity: the authors are incouraged to improve the discussion over the limitations.
reviews.questions:
1) It would be useful if the authors have any comments on the failure modes of the proposed model. For example, one of the aspects may be the confounding factors (Bontempelli et al (2022)) which could be picked up by the model? 

2) One of the perceived limitations is that the support vectors only represent the whole image. It does not allow to pick up the particular aspects of the image which takes the decision. For example, we can guess from an image of a cat with antlers that these are the antlers which make it look like a deer. But strictly speaking we don't know if such changes result in confirmation bias or whether the model actually predicts the results based on the antlers. I wonder if the authors can comment on such limitation?

3) I could imagine such work is also linked with incremental/lifelong learning, for example in a way similar to Laskov et al (2006), which considers incremental SVM. In contrast to the standard deep-learning solutions, KKT can be updated recursively, which provides a wealth of options for improving the performance. This might be an additional benefit of the proposed formulation. I wonder if the authors can comment on this? 

Bontempelli et al (2022) Concept-level Debugging of Part-Prototype Networks, ICLR 2022

Laskov, P., Gehl, C., Krüger, S., Müller, K.R., Bennett, K.P. and Parrado-Hernández, E., 2006. Incremental support vector learning: Analysis, implementation and applications. Journal of machine learning research, 7(9).
reviews.limitations: As discussed above, the section on limitations would greatly improve the discussion.
